title: January Staff Pick: Vinterhamn by Anders Lilja
content: Every January I choose a book that feels like the month itself - slow, interior, demanding. Anders Lilja's Vinterhamn is exactly that. Set on the Swedish west coast in the 1970s, it follows a retired lighthouse keeper through one long winter of memory and loss. The prose is spare but devastating. Lilja trusts silence the way a poet does. I have pressed this book into the hands of at least six customers already this week and every one of them has come back to thank me.
excerpt: Lucia's first staff pick of the year - a quiet, devastating novel about a lighthouse keeper and a winter that refuses to end.

title: Poetry Night: February at Weaver & Book
content: We are hosting our monthly poetry evening on Thursday 16 February at 18:30. This month we will be reading from Nora Clement's newly translated collection Blå stunder - the first Swedish edition of her Norwegian originals. Nora will be joining us in person and will read in both Norwegian and Swedish. Seats are limited to 20; please email us at bokhandel@weaverandbook.se to reserve your place. Wine and coffee will be served.
excerpt: Join us on 16 February for a poetry evening with author Nora Clement reading from her translated collection Blå stunder.

title: Interview: Sofia Nyman on Writing the Body in Nordic Fiction
content: Sofia Nyman sat down with me and Professor Astrid Lindén last month after her reading here at the shop. We talked for nearly two hours - about her new novel, about the tradition of embodied writing in Swedish literature, and about what it means to write a female body in a country that thinks it has already solved gender. The transcript below has been lightly edited. Sofia: I never start with a story. I start with a sensation - something physical that will not leave me alone. Astrid: That is very much in the lineage of Stridsberg, isn't it? The body as archive.
excerpt: A wide-ranging conversation with Sofia Nyman and Professor Astrid Lindén about embodied writing and the Nordic literary tradition.

title: New Arrivals: Spring 2023
content: The spring parcels have arrived and the shelves are looking wonderful. Here is what we are most excited about: Marta Kowalska's debut novel in Swedish translation from Granris Förlag, a new poetry collection from the Oslo-based press Brygge, three backlist titles we have been chasing for months from Ekblad Förlag, and the long-awaited Swedish edition of a German novel we have been recommending in its English translation since last year. Come in and browse - we have put the new arrivals table right by the window.
excerpt: Spring has arrived at Weaver & Book, and so have some wonderful new titles. Here is what just landed on our shelves.

title: Linnea's Pick: A Coming-of-Age Novel That Gets It Right
content: I started this novel on a Tuesday evening and finished it at two in the morning on Wednesday. It is narrated by a seventeen-year-old in a small Swedish town who is trying to understand why her best friend has stopped speaking to her. The author avoids every cliché you would expect - no melodrama, no redemptive arc, just the grinding, specific confusion of being young and not yet knowing how to read other people. I cried twice, which I will not specify further. It is on the staff picks table near the door.
excerpt: Linnea recommends a debut novel about friendship, silence, and the peculiar cruelty of adolescence.

title: Summer Reading List 2023
content: Every summer I put together a list that balances the pleasurable and the challenging - books you can read on a beach and books that will follow you home. This year's list includes five titles: a Swedish crime novel that is really an essay about class, a translated Greek family saga, a slim Norwegian novella about mushroom foraging that made me cry (a pattern is emerging), Marta Kowalska's debut which we wrote about in spring, and a collection of essays on landscape and memory by a writer I have been following for ten years. Full descriptions below.
excerpt: Lucia's summer reading list for 2023: five books that will please on holiday and haunt you after.

title: Weaver & Book Turns Twelve
content: Twelve years ago I signed the lease on Svartbäcksgatan 12 with a mixture of terror and absolute certainty. I had left a job in publishing in Stockholm, moved back to Uppsala, and told everyone I was opening a bookshop. Most people were politely sceptical. Some were not polite about it. Today we are still here, we have an embarrassingly loyal group of regular customers, and the shelves look exactly as I imagined them - slightly overstocked, slightly chaotic, entirely full of books I believe in. Thank you for twelve years.
excerpt: A love letter to twelve years of Weaver & Book, our customers, and the books that made it worth it.

title: Author Evening: Anders Lilja in Conversation
content: We are very pleased to welcome Anders Lilja back to Uppsala for an evening of reading and conversation on 14 October. Anders will read from Vinterhamn and from his forthcoming novel, and will then be in conversation with Professor Astrid Lindén of Uppsala University. The evening begins at 18:00. Entrance is free and open to all. Books will be available for purchase and signing. The shop will be rearranged for seating so please arrive a few minutes early.
excerpt: Anders Lilja returns to Weaver & Book on 14 October for a reading and conversation with Professor Astrid Lindén.

title: Prize Season 2023: Our Picks from the Longlists
content: The autumn prize season is upon us and the longlists are full of books we are genuinely delighted to see recognised. From the Swedish August Prize longlist we are especially excited about two titles - one a novel about a rural midwife in 1940s Norrland, the other a poetry collection that stopped me in the stockroom when I was unpacking it. From the Nordic Council's Literature Prize, we are rooting for the Icelandic title in translation. We have all the longlisted titles in stock or on order.
excerpt: Linnea picks through the 2023 prize longlists and flags the titles she is most excited about.

title: Christmas Gift Guide 2023
content: Buying a book as a gift requires a small act of trust - that you know enough about the person to choose something that will surprise them pleasantly rather than land on a pile of unread things. Here are our suggestions organised by reader type. For the reader who says they have no time: a collection of linked short stories you can finish in two evenings. For the person who wants to understand Sweden: the new biography of Astrid Lindgren. For the teenager who loved fantasy but is ready for something stranger: Nora Clement's Blå stunder. For the friend who reads everything: Anders Lilja's Vinterhamn, because they probably haven't read it yet.
excerpt: Lucia's Christmas gift guide 2023 - books for every kind of reader in your life.

title: Christmas Opening Hours 2023
content: We will be open through the Christmas period as follows: 18 December-22 December: 10:00-19:00 daily. 23 December: 10:00-17:00. 24 December (Christmas Eve): 10:00-14:00. 25-26 December: closed. 27-30 December: 11:00-17:00. 31 December: 11:00-15:00. 1 January: closed. We reopen for normal hours on 2 January. If you are looking for last-minute gift wrapping, come in on the 23rd - we will have the wrapping table set up and staff on hand to help.
excerpt: Our opening hours for Christmas and the New Year period.

title: Staff Picks: What We Read Over Christmas
content: Lucia read a 900-page Austrian novel and claims it was restful. I read four slim books in four days and one of them made me call my mother. Here are our holiday reads. Lucia: the Austrian novel was Josef Winkler and yes, restful is the wrong word, but I needed something that demanded my full attention. Linnea: I read the Kowalska debut again, slower this time, and caught things I had missed. Also a book about the history of Advent calendars which is genuinely fascinating.
excerpt: Lucia and Linnea share what they read during the Christmas break.

title: Publisher Spotlight: Granris Förlag
content: Granris Förlag is a small Stockholm-based independent press that has been publishing challenging literary fiction since 2008. They publish about six titles a year, always in small print runs, always with extraordinary cover design. We stock everything they publish and we have been known to order in backlist titles on request. Their spring catalogue this year includes a new translation of a Polish novelist we have been hoping someone would translate, and a debut from a Finnish-Swedish author that we have already pre-read in manuscript. We spoke to their editorial director, Maja Söderström, about what Granris looks for in a manuscript.
excerpt: A spotlight on Granris Förlag, the independent Stockholm press behind some of our favourite books.

title: Essay: Why I Still Believe in the Bookshop
content: People ask me often - sometimes kindly, sometimes with a hint of disbelief - how a small independent bookshop survives in 2024. The honest answer is: with difficulty, and with community, and with an insistence on knowing what we are selling. We are not a warehouse. We do not stock everything. We stock what we have read, or what we have been persuaded to read by people we trust. Every book on these shelves was put there by a human being who thought it was worth your time. I believe that still matters. I think it might matter more now than it did ten years ago.
excerpt: Lucia on what keeps an independent bookshop alive - and why she thinks the human curation still matters.

title: German Fiction at Weaver & Book
content: We have quietly built one of the best selections of German-language fiction in Uppsala. It started because Lucia lived in Berlin for two years before opening the shop and came back with a stack of books she could not stop talking about. We now stock originals and translations, and we can usually order in anything from the major German-language publishers within a week. This post is a reading guide to our current German fiction section: from Jenny Erpenbeck's back catalogue to a very recent debut that won the Leipzig Book Fair prize.
excerpt: A reading guide to our German fiction section - translations, originals, and Lucia's Berlin years.

title: Interview: Professor Astrid Lindén on the Nordic Novel
content: I have known Astrid Lindén for three years now - she first came into the shop looking for a book she remembered from a seminar in the 1990s and we spent an hour tracking it down together. Since then she has co-hosted two author evenings with us, written a guest post on Sara Stridsberg, and introduced more customers to Finnish literature than anyone else I know. We sat down in the back of the shop on a rainy May afternoon to talk about the state of Nordic fiction. Astrid: The Nordic novel is in a strange and exciting position right now. There is a generation of writers in their thirties who have completely dismantled the crime novel as a template and are doing something much more formally ambitious with it.
excerpt: Lucia talks with Professor Astrid Lindén about the current state of Nordic fiction and the writers to watch.

title: Summer Reading List 2024
content: For summer 2024 I have tried to choose books that reward slow reading - books that can sit on your nightstand for a week without losing their thread. The list this year is: an English novel shortlisted for a major prize that I initially resisted and then loved entirely; a Swedish novel in five connected stories about a family over four generations; a translated Italian essay collection about cities and memory; a Norwegian debut that is either a ghost story or a story about grief (possibly both); and a reissue of a classic Swedish novel from 1956 that has been out of print for too long.
excerpt: Five books for a summer of slow, rewarding reading - chosen by Lucia.

title: Lease Renewed: We Are Staying on Svartbäcksgatan
content: I can now tell you something I have been sitting on for three months: we have renewed our lease on Svartbäcksgatan 12. For a while it was genuinely uncertain and I want to be honest about that - commercial rents in Uppsala have risen sharply and our landlord had other options. In the end we reached an agreement and we are here for another five years. I am relieved in a way that is physical, like putting down something heavy. Thank you to everyone who came in during those months without knowing anything was at stake. You kept us going.
excerpt: The shop is staying. Lucia shares the news of the lease renewal and what the uncertainty felt like.

title: Prize Season 2024: The August Prize Longlist
content: The August Prize longlist was announced this morning and we are very pleased with it. Three of the eight longlisted titles are books we have been recommending since the spring. The fiction longlist includes a novel we have been quietly certain about since we received the advance copy in April - a multigenerational story set in Gothenburg's shipbuilding industry. The non-fiction list includes a book about Swedish language policy that sounds dry but is absolutely riveting. We have all titles in stock.
excerpt: Linnea reacts to the August Prize longlist - three of the eight titles are ones we have championed since spring.

title: Translation in Focus: What We Owe to Translators
content: We sell a lot of translated literature - probably sixty percent of our shelves are translations of one kind or another. I have been thinking about how rarely we name the translators. A novel by Marta Kowalska is often just shelved under Kowalska, with the translator's name in small print on the copyright page. This feels wrong to me. Translation is not transcription. The translator makes decisions on every page - about register, rhythm, the exact weight of a word. We are now adding translator names to our handwritten shelf notes for all translated titles. It is a small thing but it feels right.
excerpt: Lucia on why Weaver & Book is starting to credit translators more prominently - and what that means for how we think about books.

title: Christmas Gift Guide 2024
content: We have been writing these gift guides for five years and I still find them difficult in the best way - there are always more books I want to include than space allows. This year's guide is organised around a single principle: the best book for someone is the one that surprises them. For the reader who thinks they only read crime: try them on a literary thriller that dissolves into pure grief by chapter four. For the person who says they only read non-fiction: press the Kowalska into their hands and wait. The full list is below with descriptions and our honest opinions.
excerpt: Lucia's 2024 Christmas gift guide - organised around the principle of the unexpected right book.

title: New Arrivals: Winter 2025
content: January is a good month for booksellers. People have time and they have been given gift cards and they come in with a willingness to be guided that is rarer in busier months. Here is what we have just shelved: a debut novel from a Finnish author published by Ekblad Förlag that we have been excited about since the autumn fair, a reissue of a 1970s Swedish feminist classic that deserves to be back in print, two poetry collections that arrived last week and which I have not yet stopped reading, and the English translation of a German novel that Lucia has been pressing on German-speaking customers in the original for two years.
excerpt: Linnea runs through the new January arrivals - debuts, reissues, poetry, and a long-awaited translation.

title: Draft: Nordic Fiction Reading Group - Possible Series
content: DRAFT - not for publication yet. I am considering running a reading group focussed on Nordic fiction - one book every six weeks, held in the shop, guided but not led by me. Possible titles for the first year: Vinterhamn, something from the Icelandic tradition (possibly Auður Ava Ólafsdóttir?), a Finnish novel in translation, and one contemporary Danish title. Maximum twelve participants. Need to think about whether to charge or run it free. Notes for later.
excerpt: Working notes for a possible Nordic fiction reading group at the shop.

title: Poetry Evening: Sara Stridsberg and the Language of Crisis
content: On Wednesday 12 March we are hosting an evening discussion on Sara Stridsberg's poetry and dramatic writing, introduced by Professor Astrid Lindén. The evening is timed to coincide with the new Swedish edition of Stridsberg's collected early poems. Professor Lindén will give a twenty-minute introduction, followed by readings and open discussion. We will have copies of the collected poems and the three novels available for purchase. The evening starts at 19:00. Free entry, no reservation needed.
excerpt: An evening on Sara Stridsberg's work, introduced by Professor Astrid Lindén - 12 March at Weaver & Book.

title: Draft: Essay on Bookshop Interiors
content: DRAFT. Thinking through an essay about what makes a bookshop feel right. The shelving, the light, the sound. Our shop is narrow - about 45 square metres - and the narrowness forces a kind of concentration. You cannot be in the shop without being close to books. I want to write about this but I have not found the right angle yet. Maybe something about attention? The architecture of browsing?
excerpt: Working notes for an essay about the physical experience of a bookshop.

title: Summer Reading List 2025
content: My criteria for a summer reading list: the books should be readable in heat, should not demand absolute silence, but should be worth returning to in autumn and finding that they have changed. This year: a new Swedish novel about an archivist who discovers a cache of letters that rewrite what she thought she knew about her family; an English debut novel set in a lighthouse (I know - but it is extraordinary); a translated Norwegian essay collection; a short story collection from a Granris Förlag author we have championed for three years; and Nora Clement's second collection, which arrived last week and is already on the staff picks table.
excerpt: Five books for a summer of reading - Lucia's 2025 list, from archives to lighthouses.

title: New Arrivals: March 2026
content: March brings a good wave of titles and we have been unpacking boxes all week. Highlights from this delivery: a prize-winning German novel now in Swedish translation published by Ekblad Förlag, a new collection from an Uppsala-based poet who read here last year, two backlist titles we have been trying to get hold of for months, and the English paperback of a novel Lucia reviewed for a literary magazine earlier this year. We have put together a front-of-shop display around the theme of water and cities - come in and have a look.
excerpt: A fresh delivery of titles - the March 2026 new arrivals at Weaver & Book.

content: Every season the shop returns to what it loves. This week: vinterns debutanter. 'Det som stannar kvar' by Marta Kowalska (Granris Förlag) sits at the centre of the table. Come read with us on Svartbäcksgatan.
